event_blocks.summary: The 2002 FIFA World Cup Final between Germany and Brazil commences with an aerial view of the International Stadium in Yokohama, the scoreboard reading 'GER 0 0 BRA' amidst a vibrant, cheering crowd and prominent field advertisements for MasterCard, Adidas, Toshiba, and Yokohama. Early action sees Brazilian player number 17, identified as Ronaldo, dribbling skillfully but narrowly missing a shot wide of the goal, a moment of frustration for the Brazilian coach. German goalkeeper Oliver Kahn, number 1, makes a crucial save from Ronaldo, with commentators praising Kahn's duel with the Brazilian striker. The Brazilian team, in yellow, presses forward, with Ronaldinho setting up Ronaldo, whose shot is wide. Later, Brazilian player number 17, identified as Cleverson, executes a powerful header that goes just wide of the post, despite the efforts of German defender Metzelder and goalkeeper Kahn. Commentators note the absence of the suspended Michael Ballack and highlight Cleverson as a rising star who displaced Juninho. The German coach, Rudi Voeller, watches intently with a concerned expression as Brazil continues to probe, with Ronaldo and Ronaldinho involved in the attacking plays.

German goalkeeper (number 1).description: Wearing a green jersey, diving to his right to make a save, showing intense focus and athletic effort.

German coach Rudi Voeller.description: Wearing a brown jacket, standing in the stands, observing the field with a tense and concerned expression.

Field advertisements.description: Brands such as MasterCard, Adidas, Toshiba, and Yokohama are visible along the perimeter of the pitch.

Match scoreboard.description: Displays 'GER 0 0 BRA' with the 2002 FIFA World Cup logo, visible during the aerial view of the field.

Brazilian player (number 17).description: Visually wearing a yellow jersey with blue shorts, actively dribbling the ball and executing a powerful header. The commentator identifies him as 'Cleverson,' a 'rising star' who 'displaced Juninho from the starting lineup,' and describes his shot as 'ambitious' but going 'wide of the target.' He is later seen controlling the ball under pressure, with the commentator again identifying him as 'Cleverson' and mentioning 'Brazil probing.'

Brazilian team lineup.description: Displayed on screen with names including Cafu (C), Ronaldo, Ronaldinho, and coach Luiz Felipe Scolari. The commentator notes Brazil is featuring in the final for a seventh time, despite having 'uncharacteristic issues even qualifying for the competition'.

German team lineup.description: Displayed on screen with names including Oliver Kahn (C), Thomas Linke, Miroslav Klose, and coach Rudi Voeller. The commentator notes Germany is featuring in the final for a seventh time, despite having 'uncharacteristic issues even qualifying for the competition'.

Stadium crowd.description: A large, animated crowd of fans waving flags and cheering, filling the stands, adding to the intense atmosphere.

Brazilian player (Ronaldo).description: Wearing a yellow jersey with 'RONALDO' printed on the back, reacting to the play with visible emotion and focus.

Brazilian coach.description: Wearing a white polo shirt with a Nike logo and the Brazilian crest, gesturing passionately on the sidelines.

German defender (number 2).description: Wearing a white jersey with black shorts, attempting to block a shot near the goal line, showing defensive intensity.

raw_segments: The segment begins with an upbeat, driving electronic music track as a split-screen displays a close-up of a Brazilian player in a yellow jersey on the right and a green background with the hashtag #WorldCupAtHome on the left. The visual transitions quickly to a Brazilian player in a yellow jersey with the number 17 dribbling the ball, overlaid with 'GERMANY', then to a German goalkeeper in a green jersey, number 1, celebrating with 'BRAZIL' overlaid. A wide shot reveals a packed stadium with fans waving flags as a male commentator welcomes viewers to the "17th FIFA World Cup Final," noting both Germany and Brazil are featuring for a "seventh time." Graphics display the German team lineup, including Oliver Kahn (C) and Miroslav Klose, followed by the Brazilian team lineup, featuring Cafu (C), Ronaldo, and Ronaldinho. The commentator highlights this as the "first ever meeting between these footballing superpowers on the World Cup stage" at the "International Stadium in Yokohama." Teams are then seen shaking hands on the field, and a close-up shows German coach Rudi Voeller intently watching. The match kicks off with a wide aerial view of the field, showing players in their positions, the scoreboard displaying 'GER 0 0 BRA' and the 2002 FIFA World Cup logo, all while the music continues and crowd sounds become more prominent, punctuated by a whistle. The commentator declares, "So, here we go, the conclusion to a captivating month," mentioning "plenty of shocks" but ending with "two perennial contenders going toe-to-toe." The camera follows the ball as German players in white and Brazilian players in yellow move dynamically across the field, with stadium advertisements for MasterCard, Adidas, and Toshiba visible. The commentator adds that both teams had "uncharacteristic issues even qualifying for the competition," but their "quality endures," leading to a "fitting final." As crowd cheers and chants swell, a "Brazil free kick" is launched long, with Germany attempting to "equal Brazil's record."

The minute opens with a close-up of a Brazilian player (number 17) in a yellow jersey and blue shorts, skillfully dribbling the ball while being closely marked by a German defender in a white jersey. A commentator describes the early action, mentioning "Neville" being "clipped by Gilberto Silva" as Germany attempts to advance. The camera then pulls back to a wide aerial view of the 2002 FIFA World Cup match between Germany and Brazil, with the scoreboard displaying 'GER 0 0 BRA', set against the backdrop of a large, animated stadium crowd whose cheers are clearly audible. Brazilian players in yellow and blue move dynamically through midfield, while German players in white and black, identified by the commentator as "Rudy Voller's German side," attempt to intercept. The commentator notes "Schneider getting it back here and delivering the cross with closer calling forward in the middle," followed by "the important intervention of Edmilson" to divert danger. The play intensifies near the German goal, where a Brazilian player takes a shot that is saved by the German goalkeeper (number 1), wearing a green jersey, who dives to his right. The commentator identifies him by name as "Marcos." A close-up shows the frustrated goalkeeper, followed by German coach Rudi Voeller, in a brown jacket, observing from the stands with a tense expression. Brazil regains possession, and the ball is passed into the German penalty area. The commentator describes "Ronaldinho slipping it through" to "Ronaldo," who is then seen in a close-up wearing his yellow jersey with 'RONALDO' on the back. Ronaldo attempts a shot, which the commentator exclaims "is in and it's wide," adding that it was "barely believably wide from Ronaldo" who "would surely have expected to open the scoring here." The sequence ends with a reverse angle of the goalkeeper’s save, and a Brazilian coach in a white polo shirt gesturing passionately. Field advertisements for brands like MasterCard, Adidas, Toshiba, and Yokohama are visible along the pitch perimeter throughout the segment.

The minute opens with the Brazilian coach, identified by commentators as Scolari, wearing a white polo shirt with a Nike logo and the Brazilian crest, gesturing passionately on the sidelines as his contract status is discussed, with commentators noting he'll be 'unemployed tomorrow' regardless of the match outcome. The scene quickly shifts to a wide aerial view of the pitch, where Brazilian player number 17, wearing a yellow jersey and blue shorts, is seen dribbling the ball with agility, evading German defenders. Commentators describe this play as Ronaldinho delivering a 'beautiful ball over the top for Ronaldo,' who, despite the setup, 'couldn't quite get his shot away.' The camera follows the ball into the German penalty area, leading to a shot on goal. German goalkeeper number 1, identified as Oliver Kahn, wearing a green jersey, dives intensely to his right in an effort to save, with commentators praising his ability to get 'the better of the duel with Ronaldo' and noting his impressive record against other players in the World Cup. A close-up captures Kahn’s focused expression during the dive, followed by a reverse-angle replay showing Brazilian player number 17 (Ronaldo) being tackled near the goal line, with a German defender (number 2, in a white jersey) attempting to block the shot as the Brazilian player maintains control. The camera then cuts to a wider view of the field, showing the German team regrouping and the Brazilian team pressing forward. The scoreboard remains 'GER 0 0 BRA' with the 2002 FIFA World Cup logo visible, alongside field advertisements for brands like MasterCard, Adidas, Toshiba, and Yokohama. The animated stadium crowd, with fans waving flags and cheering, adds to the electric atmosphere. Commentators continue to discuss the game, mentioning other players like Trost, Frings, Nielsen, Klose, Neville, Brazilian captain Cafu, and German player Jens Jeremies, as the action builds towards another potential strike. The sequence ends with a close-up of German coach Rudi Voeller, wearing a brown jacket, observing the field with a tense and concerned expression, reflecting the high pressure of the match.

The segment opens with a close-up of a tense German player, as the commentator describes a previous play that 'needed to be spectacular' but went 'wide of the target.' A Brazilian player, number 17, identified by the commentator as 'Cleverson,' then executes a powerful header towards the goal. The German goalkeeper, number 1, dives to his right in an intense effort to save, but the ball narrowly misses the post, going 'just wide' despite the presence of a German defender, 'Metzelder,' as noted by the commentator. The commentator also mentions the absence of 'the suspended Michael Ballack,' who 'would have fared better.' The camera cuts to the German bench, showing coach Rudi Voeller with a concerned expression and other staff members with clasped hands. A wide aerial view of the pitch displays the 'GER 0 0 BRA' scoreboard for the 2002 FIFA World Cup, with players actively moving. The Brazilian team, in yellow jerseys, maintains possession, with Ronaldo, number 9, reacting with focus. The Brazilian coach, in a white polo shirt, gestures passionately on the sidelines. The commentator highlights 'Cleverson' as a 'rising star' who 'displaced Juninho from the starting lineup,' and also references 'Ronaldinho' being 'set out for semi-final.' The action continues with Cleverson dribbling past a German defender, number 2, near the sideline, showcasing agility. Field advertisements for Toshiba, Fujifilm, and MasterCard are visible as the ball moves across the field. The animated crowd adds to the electric atmosphere, while the commentator notes 'Brazil probing.' The sequence concludes with Cleverson, number 17, continuing to control the ball under pressure, with the German defense scrambling to regain control, and the commentator mentioning 'Ronaldinho once more.'

event_blocks.summary: In the 2002 FIFA World Cup final, with the scoreboard reading 'GER 0 0 BRA', Brazilian player number 15, Cleberson, unleashed a powerful shot near the penalty area that struck the crossbar, described by commentators as an "unlucky" attempt that beat the German goalkeeper, Oliver Kahn. Ronaldinho then passed to Roberto Carlos, who found Ronaldo (number 9) near the goal line; Ronaldo's subsequent shot was spectacularly saved by Kahn's legs, followed by two more diving saves from Kahn. German coach Rudi Voeller reacted with concern from the stands as commentators noted Germany was "beginning to lead a charmed life," while the Brazilian coach gestured passionately on the sidelines. After the "last action of the first half," Kahn, visibly frustrated and exhausted, sat on the ground, having conceded that Ronaldo had "a hat-trick of presentable chances" without scoring. The match continued with Brazilian player number 17 dribbling past German defender number 2, leading to another shot from Brazilian player number 15, saved by the German goalkeeper (identified as Marcos) to his right, followed by a second save to his left on a shot from Brazilian player number 11. Commentators noted that a previous attempt by Brazil's number nine was denied, keeping the "final remains deadlocked at nil-nil." German defender number 2 and Brazilian player number 9 were near the goal line during the rebound play. German coach Rudi Voeller again reacted with concern, as commentary observed "Voller's men" were not solely defending. The Brazilian coach gestured passionately. A German attack saw Jens Jeremies get a header away, deflected by Mielson's outstretched leg. The segment also highlighted Brazilian coach 'Skolari' gesturing passionately, with commentators noting 'Skolari's men been pinned back a bit since the restart.' Marcos made a "spectacular" save on a "stunning swerving strike" from Brazilian player number 15, touching it onto the post, with commentators praising his "brilliant effort." German defender number 2 was seen near the goal line. Ronaldo reacted with visible emotion. German coach Rudi Voeller was in the stands, reacting with concern, as commentators mentioned the player who scored their late winner in the round of 16 was "so close here to opening the scoring in the final." The stadium crowd was animated, waving flags and cheering. Later, around the 67th minute, German player number 19, Didi Hamann, intercepted and took a powerful but inaccurate shot, which German goalkeeper Marcos watched over as it went wide. Brazilian player number 15's follow-up shot was blocked by German defender number 2. The Brazilian coach gestured passionately. Torsten Frings and Bernd Schneider were mentioned, with Schneider identified as the most creative German player. A near-miss occurred when a German player almost connected with his Leverkusen teammate, Neuville. Lucio of Brazil was identified. Ronaldo went down but quickly got back up to win the ball back amidst visible field advertisements for Toshiba, Gillette, McDonald’s, Coca-Cola, and JVC.

German goalkeeper (number 1).description: Identified by the commentator as Marcos, wearing a green jersey with white gloves, making a diving save to his right, showing intense focus and athletic effort, as the commentator notes him 'watching it over'.

Brazilian player (number 11).description: Wearing a yellow jersey with blue shorts, attempting a follow-up shot after the initial save, showing agility and persistence.

German coach Rudi Voeller.description: Wearing a brown jacket, standing in the stands, reacting with concern and tension to the play.

Field advertisements.description: Brands such as Toshiba, Gillette, McDonald’s, Coca-Cola, and JVC are visible along the perimeter of the pitch.

Brazilian player (number 9).description: Wearing a yellow jersey with blue shorts, seen near the goal line during the save sequence, and mentioned by the commentator as having been 'denied' in an earlier play.

Match scoreboard.description: Displays 'GER 0 0 BRA' with the 2002 FIFA World Cup logo, visible during the aerial view of the field, later updating to '67 MINS'.

Brazilian coach.description: Wearing a white polo shirt with a Nike logo and the Brazilian crest, gesturing passionately on the sidelines, showing emotional engagement.

Brazilian player (number 15).description: Wearing a yellow jersey with blue shorts, attempting a follow-up shot after the initial save, showing determination and emotion.

Stadium crowd.description: A large, animated crowd of fans waving flags and cheering, filling the stands, adding to the intense atmosphere, with their constant noise, cheers, and whistles audible throughout the segment.

German defender (number 2).description: Wearing a white jersey with black shorts, blocking a shot near the goal line, showing defensive intensity.

Brazilian player (Ronaldo).description: Wearing a yellow jersey with 'RONALDO' printed on the back, reacting to the play with visible emotion and focus.

Brazilian player (number 17).description: Wearing a yellow jersey with blue shorts, actively dribbling the ball on the field, showcasing agility and control.

German player (number 19).description: Identified by the commentator as Didi Hamann, a Liverpool player, wearing a white jersey with black shorts, intercepting the ball and advancing toward the Brazilian goal, displaying offensive intent as he takes a powerful shot.

raw_segments: Amidst the electric atmosphere of the 2002 FIFA World Cup, with the scoreboard displaying 'GER 0 0 BRA' and the crowd erupting in cheers, Brazilian players in yellow jerseys advance the ball into the German half. Commentators identify Brazilian player number 15 as Cleberson, who takes a powerful shot near the penalty area, described as "unlucky" as it "corrects it against the crossbar," with the German goalkeeper, Oliver Kahn, noted as having been "beaten by that." The commentary highlights Cleberson's recent debut and "impact." The play continues with Ronaldinho passing to Roberto Carlos, who attempts to flash a shot in, finding Brazilian player number 9, Ronaldo, near the goal line. Ronaldo's follow-up shot, which commentators state usually finds the back of the net, is spectacularly "saved by the legs of Oliver Kahn," who makes two consecutive diving saves – first to his right, then to his left – showcasing intense focus. The German coach Rudi Voeller, in a brown jacket, reacts with concern from the stands, as commentators remark on his "side beginning to lead a charmed life." The Brazilian coach gestures passionately on the sidelines. After the frantic "last action of the first half," Oliver Kahn, visibly frustrated, stands up before being seen sitting on the ground, exhausted. Commentators reflect on Ronaldo having had "a hat-trick of presentable chances" in the first half without converting any. The field is surrounded by advertisements for brands like Toshiba, Gillette, McDonald’s, Coca-Cola, and JVC, while the animated crowd, filled with yellow-clad fans, continues to cheer.

The minute opens with a Brazilian player (number 17) in a yellow jersey skillfully dribbling near the sideline, evading a German defender (number 2). The ball is passed into the German penalty area, where Brazilian player (number 15) takes a powerful shot. The German goalkeeper (number 1), in a green jersey, makes a diving save to his right, but the ball rebounds. Brazilian player (number 11) quickly follows up with another shot, which is again saved by the goalkeeper diving to his left. Commentary notes that a previous attempt by 'Brazil's number nine' was denied, and the 'final remains deadlocked at nil-nil'. A wide aerial view shows the match scoreboard displaying 'GER 0 0 BRA' and the 2002 FIFA World Cup logo, with field advertisements for Toshiba, McDonald’s, Coca-Cola, and JVC visible. The stadium is alive with the sound of cheering fans, a 'very congested penalty area' is noted by the commentator, and the Brazilian fans, a sea of yellow, wave flags passionately. The camera returns to the field, showing German defender (number 2) and Brazilian player (number 9) near the goal line during the rebound play. The German goalkeeper (number 1) is then seen sitting exhausted on the ground after his intense saves. German coach Rudi Voeller, in a brown jacket, reacts with concern from the stands, as commentary observes that 'Voller's men' were not just sitting back in defense. The Brazilian coach, in a white polo shirt, gestures passionately on the sidelines. The audio then describes a German attack, with 'Jens Jeremies finding the space there to get the header away', which was 'deflected away' by 'Mielson' who 'flung out that leg to divert the danger'. The sequence concludes with a close-up of Brazilian player Ronaldo, showing visible emotion and focus, as 'Schneider' is mentioned going down, all contributing to the electric atmosphere under the stadium lights.

The segment opens with a close-up of the Brazilian coach, identified by commentators as 'Skolari,' wearing a white polo shirt with the Brazilian crest and a Nike logo, gesturing passionately on the sidelines, showing intense emotional engagement as commentators discuss winning a free kick and note 'Skolari's men been pinned back a bit since the restart.' The scene quickly shifts to a wide aerial view of the pitch during the 2002 FIFA World Cup match between Germany and Brazil, with the scoreboard displaying 'GER 0 0 BRA' and field advertisements for Toshiba, Gillette, McDonald’s, Coca-Cola, and JVC visible. The Brazilian team, in yellow jerseys and blue shorts, is seen advancing the ball, with Brazilian player number 17 dribbling skillfully. The camera follows the play as the ball is passed into the German penalty area, where Brazilian player number 15 takes a powerful shot, accompanied by the commentator exclaiming 'winding up to have a hit!' and the sound of a powerful kick. The German goalkeeper, identified as 'Marcos' (number 1), in a green jersey and white gloves, dives to his right to make a spectacular save, pushing the ball away, which commentators praise as 'certainly spectacular,' a 'stunning swerving strike and an equally fine save from Marcos,' noting he 'just touched that onto the post.' The rebound is quickly followed up by Brazilian player number 11, who attempts another shot, but the goalkeeper dives again, this time to his left, making a second consecutive save, earning further praise for his 'brilliant effort' and 'wonderful stop.' The camera captures the goalkeeper’s exhaustion as he sits on the ground after the effort. The German defender (number 2) is seen near the goal line, attempting to block the shot. Brazilian player Ronaldo reacts with visible emotion. The German coach Rudi Voeller, in a brown jacket, is seen in the stands, reacting with concern, while commentators mention 'the man who got their late winner in the round of 16 against Paraguay' was 'so close here to opening the scoring in the final.' The stadium crowd is animated, waving flags and cheering, adding to the intense atmosphere with audible cheers and general crowd noise, as commentators reiterate 'Skolari with plenty to thank his goalkeeper for' and mention 'Cafu.' The sequence ends with a close-up of a German player, possibly the goalkeeper, running and showing frustration, followed by a shot of the Brazilian player (number 17) controlling the ball near the center circle, with the match still tied at 0-0.

The 2002 FIFA World Cup match between Germany and Brazil is finely balanced, with the scoreboard showing 'GER 0 0 BRA' and the commentator noting the final is 'finally balanced'. The stadium is electric with a large, animated crowd cheering and waving flags, their constant noise, cheers, and whistles filling the air. The action begins with a Brazilian player (number 17) dribbling near the center circle, evading German defenders. The commentator identifies Marco Bode as the play develops. The ball is passed forward, and German player (number 19), identified as Didi Hamann, a Liverpool player, intercepts and advances toward the Brazilian goal. Hamann takes a powerful shot, which the commentator notes has 'plenty of power but not the requisite accuracy'. German goalkeeper (number 1), identified as Marcos, makes a diving save to his right, with the commentator observing him 'watching it over'. The ball rebounds, and Brazilian player (number 15) attempts a follow-up shot, but it is blocked by German defender (number 2) near the goal line. The camera cuts to the Brazilian coach, wearing a white polo shirt with a Nike logo and the Brazilian crest, reacting with visible frustration and gesturing passionately on the sidelines. The scene shifts back to the field as the German team regains possession and clears the ball. The commentator mentions Torsten Frings and Bernd Schneider, describing Schneider as the one 'most likely maybe, creatively to make something happen for Germany'. There's a near-miss as a German player almost slips the ball to his Leverkusen teammate, Neuville, but 'just couldn't quite connect'. The commentator then identifies Lucio of Brazil. The scoreboard updates to '67 MINS' as the game continues with both teams pressing. Ronaldo is then identified by the commentator, going down but quickly getting 'back to his feet here to win the ball back'. Field advertisements for brands like Toshiba, Gillette, McDonald’s, Coca-Cola, and JVC are visible, and the FIFA TV logo is displayed, as the sequence ends with a wide shot of players repositioning.

event_blocks.summary: At the 67-minute mark of the 2002 FIFA World Cup final, with the scoreboard reading 'GER 0 1 BRA', Brazilian player Ronaldo, lauded for his 'predatory instincts,' scored the opening goal after German goalkeeper Oliver Kahn spilled a shot from Rivaldo. The stadium erupted with intense crowd cheering as Ronaldo celebrated, embracing a teammate, while Brazilian players, including numbers 15 and 11, rushed towards the goal, and a German defender (number 2) blocked a follow-up. On the sidelines, the Brazilian coach passionately celebrated, jumping and hugging staff, in contrast to the visible tension and frustration of German coach Rudi Voeller. Commentary highlighted Ronaldo's comeback after past heartache and injuries, noting this was Brazil's first World Cup final goal since 1970. As play resumed, German player number 19 intercepted the ball, but Brazil maintained their lead amidst visible field advertisements. Later, at 79 minutes, the scoreboard updated to 'GER 0 2 BRA' after Ronaldo scored his second goal, a 'beautiful Brazilian goal' following a skillful dribble and precise shot that the German goalkeeper (number 1) failed to save. The commentator noted Kleberson's strength, Rivaldo's awareness, and Ronaldo's precision, calling it 'Redemption.' German coach Rudi Voeller reacted with disappointment, while the Brazilian coach celebrated exuberantly. The animated stadium crowd waved flags and cheered, adding to the electric atmosphere. Brazilian players (number 15 and 11) attempted follow-up shots, and German player Gerald Asamoah's cross was cleared. A subsequent play involving Linker and Frings led to a 'smart shot on the turn' by Oliver Bierhoff, which was met with an 'equally fine save' from Brazilian goalkeeper Marcos. As the match entered its final five minutes, Brazil prepared to bring on Juninho, and Schneider won a free kick that ultimately missed everyone. Throughout the match, the cheers and general noise of the animated stadium crowd were audible, alongside visible field advertisements for Gillette, Toshiba, JVC, and Budweiser.

German player (number 19).description: Wearing a white jersey with black shorts, intercepting the ball and advancing toward the Brazilian goal, displaying offensive intent; identified by the commentator as Jeremies, who wriggled into a little bit of room to create a glimpse of goal, which was reflected just wide.

German goalkeeper (number 1).description: Wearing a green jersey with white gloves, diving to his right in an attempt to save Ronaldo's shot, but failing as the ball enters the net; later seen lying on the ground after the save attempt.

Brazilian player (number 11).description: Wearing a yellow jersey with blue shorts, attempting a follow-up shot after the initial save, showing agility and persistence.

German defender (number 2).description: Wearing a white jersey with black shorts, blocking a shot near the goal line, showing defensive intensity.

German coach Rudi Voeller.description: Wearing a brown jacket, standing in the stands, reacting with visible tension and frustration to the goal, showing concern and disappointment.

Field advertisements.description: Brands such as Gillette, Toshiba, JVC, and Budweiser are visible along the perimeter of the pitch, adding to the commercial atmosphere of the stadium.

Brazilian player (Ronaldo).description: Wearing a yellow jersey with 'RONALDO' printed on the back, celebrating the goal with arms raised and then embracing a teammate, displaying intense emotion and joy; identified by the commentator as 'Ronaldo', a striker back at the summit of the world game, scoring with a 'golden boot' for 'retribution'.

Match scoreboard.description: Displays 'GER 0 2 BRA' with the 2002 FIFA World Cup logo and '79 MINS' timer, confirming Brazil's lead, and later updating to reflect the score, with the commentator noting the match is entering its 'final five minutes'.

Brazilian coach.description: Wearing a white polo shirt with a Nike logo and the Brazilian crest, celebrating passionately on the sidelines, jumping and hugging staff, showing intense emotion.

Brazilian player (number 15).description: Wearing a yellow jersey with blue shorts, attempting a follow-up shot after the initial save, showing determination and emotion.

Stadium crowd.description: A large, animated crowd of fans waving flags and cheering, filling the stands, adding to the intense and celebratory atmosphere, with their cheers and general stadium noise audible throughout the segment.

Brazilian player (number 17).description: Wearing a yellow jersey with blue shorts, actively dribbling the ball on the field, showcasing agility and control.

raw_segments: The 2002 FIFA World Cup final between Germany and Brazil reaches a critical juncture at the 67-minute mark, with the scoreboard displaying 'GER 0 1 BRA'. The commentator exclaims, "Now Rivaldo, oh and Kahn spilled it! Ronaldo! To make the breakthrough!" as Brazilian player Ronaldo, wearing a yellow jersey with 'RONALDO' on the back, celebrates his goal with arms raised in triumph, then embraces a teammate in a joyful hug, his "predatory instincts" lauded by the commentary. The stadium erupts with intense crowd cheering. Replays show German goalkeeper Oliver Kahn, in a green jersey, diving to his right in a desperate attempt to save the shot, but the ball slips past him into the net. The commentator expresses sympathy for Kahn, noting he "couldn't hold Rivaldo's effort" despite an "excellent World Cup." Brazilian players, including numbers 15 and 11, react with excitement, rushing toward the goal, while a German defender (number 2) is seen blocking a follow-up shot near the goal line. On the sidelines, the Brazilian coach, identified as part of "Scolari's side" by the commentary, passionately celebrates in his white polo shirt, jumping and hugging staff. In stark contrast, German coach Rudi Voeller, in a brown jacket, reacts with visible tension and frustration. The commentary provides historical context, highlighting Ronaldo's comeback, stating he "is back" after "four years on from the final heartache against France, and all the injury issues that have followed," and notes this is "Brazil's first goal in a World Cup final since 1970." The vibrant crowd continues to cheer amidst visible advertisements for Gillette, Toshiba, and JVC. As play resumes, German player number 19 intercepts the ball and advances toward the Brazilian goal, with Brazilian players repositioning defensively, the atmosphere electric with Brazil's lead and Germany's urgency.

The 2002 FIFA World Cup match between Germany and Brazil is in its final 20 minutes in Yokohama, with the scoreboard showing 'GER 0 1 BRA' at 67 minutes, as the commentator notes Germany needs to show more urgency. Brazilian player Cafu is seen, followed by Kleberson (number 17), who makes a strong run, skillfully dribbling the ball down the field and evading German defenders. The camera follows the play as the ball is passed into the German penalty area. The commentator exclaims, 'Ronaldo's left it! Ronaldo scores!' as Brazilian player Ronaldo takes a powerful, precise shot that the German goalkeeper (number 1) dives to save but fails, allowing the ball to cross the line. The scoreboard updates to 'GER 0 2 BRA' at 79 minutes, confirming Brazil's second goal, which the commentator notes puts them well on their way to a record fifth World Cup success, calling it a 'beautiful Brazilian goal.' Ronaldo celebrates with arms raised, then embraces a teammate, displaying intense joy, while the commentator highlights that 'Kleberson was strong, Rivaldo was aware, and Ronaldo was precise,' adding 'Redemption.' The German coach Rudi Voeller, visible in the stands wearing a brown jacket, reacts with visible frustration and disappointment. In contrast, the Brazilian coach, in a white polo shirt with the Nike logo and Brazilian crest, celebrates passionately on the sidelines, jumping and hugging staff. The stadium crowd is animated, waving flags and cheering, adding to the electric atmosphere, with field advertisements for Gillette, Toshiba, JVC, and Budweiser visible along the pitch perimeter. The sequence continues with Brazilian player (number 15) attempting a follow-up shot after the initial save, while German defender (number 2) blocks a shot near the goal line. Brazilian player (number 11) also attempts a follow-up, showing agility and persistence. German player Jeremies (number 19) is seen intercepting the ball and advancing toward the Brazilian goal, having wriggled into a glimpse of goal that was reflected just wide. The camera captures close-ups of the German goalkeeper lying on the ground after the save attempt and a German player wiping sweat from his forehead, highlighting the physical intensity of the match.

The segment opens with a close-up of German coach Rudi Voeller, visibly tense and frustrated in the stands, as the scoreboard displays 'GER 0 2 BRA' at 79 minutes, confirming Brazil's lead in the 2002 FIFA World Cup. The commentator exclaims, "And retribution for Ronaldo, a striker back at the very summit of the world game with another swipe of that golden boot," as wide shots show Brazilian players in yellow jerseys pressing forward. A key moment unfolds as Brazilian player (number 17), identified as Ronaldo, dribbles with agility and shoots. The German goalkeeper (number 1), in a green jersey, dives to his right but fails to save the ball, which enters the net. Ronaldo, wearing his yellow jersey, celebrates with arms raised, then embraces a teammate, displaying intense joy, while the Brazilian coach reacts passionately on the sidelines, jumping and hugging staff. A whistle sounds, and the commentator notes, "A long way back from here for Germany." The action continues with German players attempting to counter; a German defender (number 2) blocks a shot, while Brazilian players (number 15 and 11) attempt follow-up shots. The commentator mentions German player Gerald Asamoah, whose cross is cleared, and then describes a play involving "Linker to Frings" and a "smart shot on the turn" by Oliver Bierhoff, which is met with an "equally fine save from the impressive Marcos," the Brazilian goalkeeper. The commentator provides background on Bierhoff's career. As the match enters its "final five minutes," Brazil is "waiting to bring on Juninho." Thomas Linker moves forward, and Schneider goes down, winning a free kick. A whistle signals the free kick, which the commentator describes as missing "everybody in the middle." Throughout, the animated stadium crowd, waving flags and cheering, contributes to the intense atmosphere, with their cheers and general noise audible, alongside visible field advertisements for brands like Gillette, Toshiba, JVC, and Budweiser. The scoreboard updates to 'GER 0 2 BRA', reinforcing Brazil's dominance.

event_blocks.summary: At the 79th minute of the 2002 FIFA World Cup final, with Brazil leading Germany 2-0, a distressed German player in a white jersey wipes his face as the scoreboard confirms the score. A commentator notes a "costly" missed opportunity by Christoph Metzelder, while Brazilian player number 17 skillfully dribbles and scores, with the German goalkeeper diving unsuccessfully. As the German goalkeeper lies in disappointment and Brazilian players begin to celebrate, the commentator calls a foul, immediately followed by the full-time whistle, declaring "Brazil are champions of the world once more." Brazilian player Ronaldo, identifiable by his name on his back, raises his arms in triumph and embraces a teammate, while the Brazilian coach celebrates passionately on the sidelines, jumping and hugging staff. The stadium crowd, a sea of yellow and green, erupts in cheers and waves flags, their shouts intensifying the electric atmosphere. The commentator elaborates on Brazil's "record extending fifth title," noting it came at Germany's expense, who suffered defeat in the final for the fourth time, and credits Ronaldo's two goals as the difference. The celebration escalates as Brazilian players lift their captain, Cafu (number 2), onto their shoulders, and he raises the FIFA World Cup trophy high above his head amidst falling confetti. The commentator highlights Cafu's historic third successive World Cup final appearance and his lifting the trophy as captain. The final frames show the entire Brazilian team on the podium, surrounded by smoke and confetti, as Cafu holds the trophy aloft, symbolizing their victory, with the commentator concluding on "Samba celebrations" for Asia's first World Cup. The atmosphere is electric, filled with joy, emotion, and national pride, underscored by constant crowd noise and cheering. The male commentator declares Brazil's triumphant return to the global stage, stating they are 'World champions again in 2002' after being runners-up four years prior. Following a brief, high-pitched digital fanfare, an upbeat, celebratory instrumental track begins, perfectly complementing the jubilant visuals. Brazilian players in yellow jerseys, including one wearing a green cap, are seen joyfully embracing and lifting the golden FIFA World Cup trophy amidst a flurry of confetti and smoke. The camera then pans to a massive, ecstatic crowd of fans, predominantly in yellow and green, waving flags and cheering, their energy amplified by the triumphant music. Brazilian player number 9, wearing a yellow jersey and green cap, proudly holds the golden trophy aloft, surrounded by beaming teammates and staff, including a player in a white 'Jesus ❤️ You' shirt. The scene is filled with emotional expressions, hugs, and celebratory gestures, capturing the peak of Brazil's victory moment. The video concludes with a green-tinted stadium view overlaid with '#WorldCupAtHome', 'SUBSCRIBE NOW', and 'WATCH NEXT', promoting further content, all set to the continuing celebratory soundtrack.
